messages.content: In your new job on the evaluation team at the Global Alliance for Clean Cook Stoves (funded by the United Nations Foundation), you’ve been asked to determine the prevalence and incidence of high blood pressure among household cooks who use traditional cook stoves living in rural Karnataka India.  Your field team is actively recruiting participants for a two-year study. As part of study enrollment, they measured blood pressure in 166 primary household cooks. 43 of them have high blood pressure at enrollment. Calculate the measure(s) of occurrence identified in question 1 and interpret in one sentence.

To calculate the measures of occurrence, we need the numbers for prevalence and incidence.

Prevalence: This refers to the proportion of the study population that already has a condition at a specific point in time.

Prevalence of high blood pressure among household cooks using traditional cook stoves = (Number of cooks with high blood pressure at enrollment / Total number of primary household cooks) x 100
Prevalence = (43 / 166) x 100 = 25.9%

Interpretation: Among the enrolled household cooks who use traditional cook stoves in rural Karnataka, 25.9% already had high blood pressure at the time of the study.

Incidence: This refers to the number of new cases of a condition that develop during a specific time period.

Since the study is for two years and we only have data at enrollment, we cannot calculate the incidence at this point. Incidence can be determined later when data is collected longitudinally over two years and new cases of high blood pressure can be identified and compared to the baseline data from enrollment.

Once enrollment was complete, the field team then followed the primary cooks without high blood pressure for 2 years, and their blood pressure is measured every 6 months. All these cooks used traditional cook stoves. During this time, 33 cooks and their families moved to a different village and were lost to follow up. Among those who remained in the study village, 15 primary cooks developed high blood pressure over the course of the study. 

I3.	One of the participants in the study asks you to tell them their individual risk of high blood pressure. What do you tell them?

To calculate the individual risk of high blood pressure for the participant, we need to know the number of participants who remained in the study village and the number of those who developed high blood pressure over the course of the study.

Participants remaining in the study village = Total participants at enrollment - Participants lost to follow up
Participants remaining = 166 - 33 = 133

Individual risk of high blood pressure = (Number of participants who developed high blood pressure / Participants remaining in the study village) x 100
Individual risk of high blood pressure = (15 / 133) x 100 = 11.3%

Therefore, you can tell the participant that their individual risk of developing high blood pressure over the course of the study is 11.3%.

I4.	Calculate the risk (including those lost to follow up) among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves.  Show your calculations and round to a whole number. Sometimes investigators calculate a risk using all those at risk at baseline (regardless of who dropped out.)

To calculate the risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves, we need to consider all the participants who were enrolled in the study at baseline, regardless of whether they were lost to follow-up.

Risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves = (Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure / Total number of primary cooks at enrollment) x 100

Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure = 15
Total number of primary cooks at enrollment = 166

Risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves = (15 / 166) x 100 = 9%

The risk of developing high blood pressure among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves, including those lost to follow-up, is approximately 9%.
how I can  round to a whole number of 0.1219512195

To round the number 0.1219512195 to a whole number, you can use standard rounding rules. 

If the decimal value is 0.5 or above, you round up to the nearest whole number. If the decimal value is below 0.5, you round down to the nearest whole number.

In this case, the decimal value is 0.1219512195. Since the decimal value is less than 0.5, you round down to 0.

Therefore, the rounded whole number for 0.1219512195 is 0.
Calculate the odds (using info from question 2) and interpret the odds of high blood pressure in your study sample in one sentence. Show your calculations.

To calculate the odds of high blood pressure in the study sample, we need to know the number of participants with high blood pressure and the number of participants without high blood pressure at enrollment.

Number of participants with high blood pressure = 43
Number of participants without high blood pressure = Total participants at enrollment - Number of participants with high blood pressure
Number of participants without high blood pressure = 166 - 43 = 123

Odds of high blood pressure = (Number of participants with high blood pressure / Number of participants without high blood pressure)
Odds of high blood pressure = 43 / 123 ≈ 0.3496

Interpretation: The odds of high blood pressure among the study sample of household cooks using traditional cook stoves in rural Karnataka is approximately 0.3496. This means that for every one cook without high blood pressure, there are 0.3496 cooks with high blood pressure.

I6.	Calculate the risk (excluding those lost to follow up) and interpret the risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks in your study sample in one sentence. Show your calculations and round to a whole number

To calculate the risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks in the study sample (excluding those lost to follow up), we need to consider the number of cooks who developed high blood pressure and the number of cooks who remained in the study village.

Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure = 15
Number of cooks remaining in the study village = Total participants at enrollment - Participants lost to follow-up
Number of cooks remaining = 166 - 33 = 133

Risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks = (Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure / Number of cooks remaining in the study village) x 100

Risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks = (15 / 133) x 100 ≈ 11.3%

Interpretation: The risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks in the study sample (excluding those lost to follow-up) is approximately 11.3%. This means that about 11.3% of the primary cooks developed high blood pressure over the course of the study.
43 of them had high blood pressure at enrollment. you should substract them.

Apologies for the incorrect calculation. You are correct. We should subtract the number of cooks with high blood pressure at enrollment from the total number of cooks remaining in the study village.

Number of cooks with high blood pressure at enrollment = 43
Number of cooks remaining in the study village = Total participants at enrollment - Participants lost to follow-up
Number of cooks remaining = 166 - 33 = 133

Number of cooks without high blood pressure = Number of cooks remaining - Number of cooks with high blood pressure at enrollment
Number of cooks without high blood pressure = 133 - 43 = 90

Risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks = (Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure / Number of cooks without high blood pressure) x 100

Risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks = (15 / 90) x 100 ≈ 16.7%

Interpretation: The risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks in the study sample (excluding those lost to follow-up) is approximately 16.7%. This means that about 16.7% of the primary cooks developed high blood pressure over the course of the study, after excluding those who already had high blood pressure at enrollment.
please round it to the whole number

Apologies for not rounding it to a whole number. 

Rounded to a whole number, the risk of high blood pressure among primary cooks in the study sample (excluding those lost to follow-up) is 17%. This means that approximately 17% of the primary cooks developed high blood pressure over the course of the study, after excluding those who already had high blood pressure at enrollment.

please calculate this section again with 43 number with high blood pressure at the beginning in mind. I4.	Calculate the risk (including those lost to follow up) among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves.  Show your calculations and round to a whole number. Sometimes investigators calculate a risk using all those at risk at baseline (regardless of who dropped out.)

Apologies for the confusion. Let's calculate the risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves while considering all participants at baseline, including those lost to follow-up.

Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure = 15
Number of cooks lost to follow-up = 33
Number of cooks at baseline = 166

Risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves (including those lost to follow-up) = (Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure / Number of cooks at baseline) x 100

Risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves = (15 / 166) x 100 ≈ 9%

Rounded to a whole number, the risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves (including those lost to follow-up) is 9%. This means that approximately 9% of the cooks using traditional cook stoves developed high blood pressure over the duration of the study, considering all participants at baseline.
no. it should be 15/123

Apologies for the previous incorrect calculations. Let's recalculate the risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves while considering all participants at baseline, including those lost to follow-up.

Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure = 15
Number of cooks without high blood pressure at enrollment = 123 (166 - 43)

Risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves (including those lost to follow-up) = (Number of cooks who developed high blood pressure / Number of cooks without high blood pressure at enrollment) x 100

Risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves = (15 / 123) x 100 ≈ 12.2%

Rounded to a whole number, the risk among cooks exposed to traditional cook stoves (including those lost to follow-up) is 12%. This means that approximately 12% of the cooks using traditional cook stoves developed high blood pressure over the duration of the study, considering all participants at baseline.
